claims.statement: rclone exposes bucket listing as first-class ls* subcommands (lsjson, lsf, ls, lsl, lsd), not only as an internal step of sync.
claims.qualification: This contradicts the inherited framing that listing is merely an internal precursor to sync; the ls* commands are dedicated listing entry points.

claims.statement: rclone's S3 backend has two distinct listing request patterns: a flat undelimited ListR and a per-directory hierarchical walk with Delimiter=/.
claims.qualification: Both patterns were run and traced on the wire at smoke scale.

claims.statement: For the ls* listing commands a plain lsjson -R is already the flat ListR; the genuine per-directory walk must be forced with --disable ListR or --max-depth, and --fast-list does not select between the two.
claims.qualification: walk.ListR, called directly by lsjson/lsf/ls/lsl, never consults ci.UseListR; the --fast-list gate lives in the separate walk.Walk entry point used by sync/copy. This corrects the inherited model that the default is a delimiter walk selected by --fast-list.

claims.statement: Forcing the walk with --disable ListR produces per-directory delimited listing chains. It was run as recursive-walk (PASS 9841/9841); a separate --dump headers probe (walk-debug) traced 13 ListObjectsV2 page requests, every one carrying delimiter=%2F, across four directory chains (the parent directory 1 page, access/ 10 continuation pages, archive/ 1, doc/ 1). The PASS run and the header trace are different executions.
claims.qualification: --disable ListR nils the ListR feature so walk.ListR falls back to the per-directory Walk.

claims.statement: Re-running a plain lsjson -R (no --fast-list) emitted zero delimiter= requests — a single undelimited chain — consistent with the source finding that a plain -R is already the flat ListR.
claims.qualification: This is an ad-hoc side-by-side trace recorded in the recursive-hierarchical receipt's correction block, not a committed --dump headers receipt, so it corroborates rather than independently confirms the runtime shape.

claims.statement: The flat ListR path issues a single undelimited ListObjectsV2 continuation chain, paged serially (list-type=2, max-keys=1000, chained by continuation-token).
claims.qualification: Traced on the wire and run as recursive-fastlist and lsf.

claims.statement: Per upstream documentation, --fast-list uses more memory but fewer transactions.
claims.qualification: This is the documented design tradeoff, not a measured study result.

claims.statement: At the source level the S3 ListR streams entries to the caller through list.NewHelper tranche flush rather than accumulating the whole object list; --fast-list's extra memory is the walker's dirMap of directory paths retained to synthesize parents.
claims.qualification: This corrects the inherited 'nothing discarded until the listing finishes' framing at the source level and rebuts the W2 allegation that --fast-list holds the whole listing in RAM; whether memory stays bounded at scale is a separate unsettled question.

claims.statement: The inherited allegation that --fast-list holds the whole listing in RAM and runs out of memory at roughly 100 million objects at the bucket root is not settled by smoke; whether --fast-list stays memory-bounded or hits an out-of-memory cliff on a deep or enormous keyspace is a scale question.
claims.qualification: Smoke peaked near 70 MB for 148,917 broad-shallow keys, a data point consistent with streaming, not a scale refutation. The streaming --fast-list listing path also differs from the v1.67-era sync path the OOM reports concern; see claim oom-exit-zero-report.

evidence.reason: No cgroup-capped, scaled, or deep-keyspace run has been performed; smoke cannot reach a memory cliff.

claims.statement: Neither request pattern shards the keyspace within a prefix; the only parallelism is the hierarchical walk fanning distinct directories across --checkers.
claims.qualification: Traced: the flat ListR is a single serial chain, and the walk's access/ directory paged serially in 10 continuation-token requests with no intra-directory sharding.

claims.statement: Within any one prefix, the default ListObjectsV2 pagination is a serial cursor-chained loop: page N+1 requires page N's continuation token.
claims.qualification: Traced on the wire as a chained continuation-token sequence; the page loop stops when IsTruncated is false.

claims.statement: The legacy ListObjects v1 path pages the same way, serially: each request carries the previous response's NextMarker or last returned key as the Marker.
claims.qualification: Source-established; v1 was smoked for completeness but its serial marker chaining was not separately header-traced, so it is source-supported rather than receipt-confirmed.

claims.statement: The S3 backend pacer keys entirely on error and retry state (LastError and ConsecutiveRetries), never on latency; on success it decays inter-request sleep and drops it to zero once the decayed value falls below minSleep.
claims.qualification: This corrects the inherited 'AIMD on delay' characterization: the pacer reacts to explicit error signals, not response latency, and it uses the S3 calculator, not the Default calculator's floor-at-minSleep behavior.

claims.statement: The pacer adapts inter-request sleep (backoff), not concurrency; listing calls are serial regardless of pacer state.
claims.qualification: This corrects the inherited claim that the pacer is an adaptive concurrency mechanism.

claims.statement: --checkers (default 8) bounds the concurrent-directory fan-out of the genuine hierarchical walk and is inert on the flat ListR.
claims.qualification: The walk's per-directory fan-out is run-traced; --checkers is the chan listJob worker-pool width. This corrects the inherited 'shouldn't affect pure listing' claim for --checkers on the walk while leaving it true for the flat path.

claims.statement: Whether a non-default --checkers count changes the genuine walk's wall-clock or request count is not settled by smoke.
claims.qualification: Only --checkers 4 (one non-default value) was run, and smoke produces no comparative timing; a checker sweep is a benchmark item.

evidence.reason: No --checkers sweep was run and smoke yields no comparative numbers.

claims.statement: --transfers (default 4) governs transfer concurrency only and has no effect on listing.
claims.qualification: Source-supported; corroborates the inherited expectation for --transfers.

claims.statement: The default --s3-list-chunk page size (max-keys=1000) was observed on the wire for ListObjectsV2.
claims.qualification: Only the default value was exercised.

claims.statement: The effect of a non-default --s3-list-chunk on request count or wall-clock is not settled by smoke.
claims.qualification: No non-default chunk value was run; AWS caps max-keys at 1000, so only values at or below it are meaningful.

claims.statement: --tpslimit and --tpslimit-burst form a separate token-bucket transactions-per-second limiter, distinct from the S3 backoff pacer, and --low-level-retries bounds retry attempts.
claims.qualification: This clarifies the inherited grouping of these flags with the pacer; the token-bucket limiter and the S3 backoff pacer are different mechanisms.

claims.statement: The lsf and lsjson structured output formats were exercised at smoke.
claims.qualification: lsjson (JSON array) and lsf (delimited) were both run and verified.

claims.statement: No rclone list command emits Parquet; listing output is JSON, delimited text, or CSV only.
claims.qualification: A documented capability gap, not a behavioral defect.

claims.statement: A constrained-memory --fast-list run under a cgroup cap on a scaled bucket has not been performed.
claims.qualification: This is the scale failure-mode test the memory and exit-code hypotheses depend on; it is deferred to the benchmark phase.

evidence.reason: No cgroup-capped constrained-memory run was performed; it is deferred to the benchmark.

claims.statement: The full-bucket fast-list smoke run sampled a 69.6 MB peak RSS for the container's main process while listing 148,917 keys.
claims.qualification: This is a main-process high-water mark from one smoke run, not a scale curve or benchmark result.

claims.statement: v1.74.4 carries the v1.70 --list-cutoff fix: directory listings above the default 1,000,000-entry threshold are sorted on disk (external sort) via github.com/lanrat/extsort rather than in RAM.
claims.qualification: This distinguishes the pinned version's listing memory behavior from the v1.67-era sync path the OOM reports concern.

claims.statement: OOM-killed rclone runs have been reported to exit with status 0 — a silent failure reported as success.
claims.qualification: Sourced from two distinct GitHub issue records by the same reporter (#7966 and #7974) alleging exit 0 on the sync path in v1.67-era code, which the pinned v1.74.4 listing path postdates; only the citation provenance was corrected and the behavior is unsettled. The full record is owned by running.md, section The exit-0-on-OOM report and its caveats.

evidence.reason: Not reproduced: no OOM was induced under a memory cap this phase and every groundwork run exited 0 normally. Settling it needs a sync-shaped workload on a scaled bucket under a cgroup memory cap with a recorded exit code, and even then it speaks to v1.74.4, not the reporter's v1.67.

claims.statement: A reported stall of more than 3 hours before transfers begin at 100 million objects concerns the sync/copy pipeline, not a pure listing command.
claims.qualification: This reclassifies the inherited stall claim: the cited issue #5859 is an open, acknowledged-by-design feature request (start transfer of objects while paginating), not a bug report about listing.

evidence.reason: Timing-to-first-transfer at scale on the sync pipeline was not run and is not a pure-listing behavior.

claims.statement: A reported roughly 7 GB resident-memory figure on large listings is an at-scale RSS claim.
claims.qualification: Cited from the closed issue #2157; smoke RSS near 70 MB is not comparable and settles nothing at scale.

evidence.reason: No scaled RSS measurement was performed.

claims.statement: Source shows no checkpoint or cursor state in Fs.list, so an interrupted lsjson would restart enumeration from scratch (rclone's resume machinery is for transfers, not listing).
claims.qualification: This is a source-level observation, not a runtime test.

claims.statement: Whether a killed listing actually restarts from zero with no crash-resume is not confirmed by a run.
claims.qualification: The source observation is recorded in claim no-list-checkpoint-state-in-source; a positive answer needs the SIGKILL-and-resume protocol.

claims.statement: Upstream rclone is a mature, widely adopted project, recorded at 58,380 GitHub stars on 2026-07-17.
claims.qualification: Star count is time-varying; it is recorded as an adoption and maturity data point, not a study behavior. The 58,380 @ 2026-07-17 figure is frozen in research/reconciliation.md rather than read live.

claims.statement: rclone supports roughly 70 storage backends, of which S3 is one.
claims.qualification: Context for the tool's scope; S3 listing is one backend among many.

claims.statement: rclone's S3 backend includes a genuine adaptive backoff pacer, a distinctive rate-control mechanism among listing tools.
claims.qualification: The mechanism exists and is distinctive; its exact control law is stated in claim s3-pacer-is-error-driven, which corrects the inherited characterization.

claims.statement: Nine lsjson invocations passed --use-server-modtime --no-mimetype to suppress the per-object HEAD — the seven verifier-checked lsjson modes plus the two capability probes — and the seven verifier-checked runs each recorded fields=0.
claims.qualification: The suppressed correct path is receipt-backed; the study never ran rclone the wrong way. The two capability probes carry the flags but are verifier-exempt, so fields=0 is recorded on the seven verified runs only.

claims.statement: By default lsjson and lsl compute ModTime and MimeType, each of which does a HEAD per object, so a naive listing of this bucket would fire 148,917 HEADs on top of the LIST requests; proper listing must pass --use-server-modtime --no-mimetype.
claims.qualification: The HEAD-storm magnitude is source-established across three functions and inferred; it was never run, so there is no receipt for the wrong way.

claims.statement: rclone has no --no-sign-request flag; it installs aws.AnonymousCredentials when no keys are set and env_auth is false, and every smoke run executed under the wrapper's credential-starved environment (auth=anonymous).
claims.qualification: Anonymity is the absence of credentials; the wrapper empties every AWS_* value and points credential/config files at a nonexistent path. Wire-level confirmation that no request carried an Authorization header is the separate claim anonymous-no-authorization-on-wire, established only by the two header-inspecting probes.

claims.statement: The two capability probes that inspect request headers (_capability/debug, _capability/walk-debug) confirmed no Authorization header on any request.
claims.qualification: Only the two --dump headers probes inspect the wire; the ordinary mode receipts record credential starvation but not request headers.

claims.statement: storage_class (lsjson's .Tier) comes straight from the ListObjectsV2 response with no per-object HEAD, and was verified equal to the manifest StorageClass on all 148,917 keys.
claims.qualification: Confirmed on the full-bucket fast-list receipt with fields=0.

claims.statement: The legacy --s3-list-version 1 (ListObjects v1, Marker paging) is a distinct list API from the default ListObjectsV2; it was smoked PASS on 2,549 keys.
claims.qualification: The inherited tool page omitted this API; it is an additive finding.

claims.statement: Whether the legacy v1 and default v2 list APIs match in wall-clock and request count at scale is not settled by smoke; both completed identically on 2,549 keys, which is not a comparative measurement.
claims.qualification: A v1-vs-v2 parity sweep is a benchmark item; smoke produces no comparative numbers.

evidence.reason: No v1-vs-v2 timing or request-count comparison was run; smoke yields no comparative numbers.

claims.statement: During the 2026-07-17 session NOAA re-uploaded objects under normals-hourly/ and normals-monthly/ (their LastModified advanced to that day), and a harness re-list returned DRIFT — a third-party bucket event, not an rclone behavior.
claims.qualification: The genuine walk was re-verified on the still-un-drifted normals-annualseasonal/1981-2010/ scope; the mtime drift was flagged to the manifest owner. This is an event record, not a tool claim.

claims.statement: At smoke scale every verifier-checked mode PASSed (0 duplicates, 0 missing, 0 extra, 0 field mismatches) and the full bucket re-listed byte-exact against the manifest of 148,917 keys.
claims.qualification: For lsjson modes the verifier checked key, size, mtime, and storage_class (etag exempt by adapter policy); lsf checked key and size only. These are single-run groundwork facts, not benchmark results.

claims.statement: rclone is trivial to install: upstream publishes ready-to-run multi-arch container images and prebuilt static binaries, and this study ran the pinned image with no local build.
claims.qualification: An ease-of-install judgment inherited from the tool page; the memory and exit-code questions still require a constrained-memory harness, conserved by claims constrained-memory-fastlist-run, fast-list-memory-at-scale, and oom-exit-zero-report.

claims.statement: The ListObjectVersions API (--s3-versions) was not smoked because the smoke bucket is unversioned.
claims.qualification: A distinct request and output shape that no run exercised; it is deferred coverage, not a settled behavior.

evidence.reason: The smoke bucket is unversioned, so the versions API was never exercised.

claims.statement: The ls, lsl, and lsd output-format commands issue the same S3 requests as lsjson and were not separately timed.
claims.qualification: They are output-format variants over the same request pattern; smoke did not run or time them separately, and smoke produces no comparative numbers regardless.

evidence.reason: ls/lsl/lsd were not run or separately timed; smoke yields no comparative numbers.
